text: The surgeon uses sharp dissection and electrocautery to take down dense intra-abdominal adhesions. The right crus of the diaphragm is dissected away from the hernia sac and skeletonized down to the confluence, while ensuring the esophagus remains unharmed.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection at the angle of His, carefully separating the tissue in this anatomical region.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to separate the gastric remnant from the gastric pouch, ensuring no injury to either structure. The parasophageal hernia is then repaired posteriorly, with both vagi identified and preserved.
The surgeon completes circumferential dissection around the esophagus, extending the dissection up to the level of the pulmonary vein to gain additional esophageal length.

The surgeon performs a collis gastroplasty due to limited intra-abdominal length of the lower esophagus. The hernia sac is completely dissected and excised. The right and left crura are approximated using interrupted non-absorbable sutures.
The surgeon trims a biologic mesh to fit around the esophagus and secures it in place with a simple, non-absorbable suture.

The anesthesiologist places a 58 French bougie to size the hiatus, which remains in place during the gastroplasty. The surgeon performs a partial gastrectomy using a 60mm linear stapler, followed by imbrication of the staple line with a running barb suture.

A bubble leak test is performed at the conclusion of the surgery, showing no evidence of leakage from the repair site. The post-operative upper GI series is displayed, indicating no presence of a hiatal hernia.
